_note: This file contains ALL user-facing strings. Medical content is based on AAP 2022, NSF 2023, and WHO guidelines. Update strings here; never in content.json.

translations.global_medical_disclaimer: This guide provides general information based on AAP, WHO, and NSF pediatric sleep guidelines. It does not replace the advice of your child's doctor. Always consult your pediatrician with specific concerns.
translations.sleep_needs_note_0_3m: Newborns sleep in short cycles of 2–4 hours around the clock. Their circadian rhythm is not yet developed. Night and day confusion is normal and improves by 6–8 weeks.
translations.sleep_needs_note_4_6m: The 4-month sleep regression is real — the brain shifts to adult-like sleep cycles. Babies may begin sleeping longer stretches at night (4–6 hours). Consistent routines help enormously.
translations.sleep_needs_note_7_9m: Most babies can sleep 9–11 hours at night with 2–3 naps. The 8–10 month regression is common due to developmental leaps (crawling, pulling to stand, object permanence).
translations.sleep_needs_note_10_12m: Two naps remain ideal. Night sleep is typically consolidated. The 12-month regression can appear as nap resistance or early waking.
translations.sleep_needs_note_13_18m: The 2-to-1 nap transition typically occurs between 13–18 months. Signs include nap refusal, short naps, or difficulty falling asleep at bedtime. Transition gradually.
translations.sleep_needs_note_19_24m: One midday nap is established. The 2-year regression is common and linked to language explosion and growing autonomy. Bedtime resistance and night waking may increase temporarily.
translations.sleep_needs_note_25_36m: Naps begin dropping between 2.5–3 years for some children. Replace with 'quiet time.' Night sleep needs remain 10–12 hours. The 3-year regression is tied to developmental and emotional growth.
translations.nap_transition_2_to_1: Transitioning from 2 naps to 1 — offer 1 nap at noon and adjust bedtime earlier on no-nap days.
translations.nap_transition_1_to_0: Transitioning from 1 nap to none — replace with quiet time and move bedtime 30–45 minutes earlier.
translations.sleep_tip_0_3m_circadian: Expose your baby to natural daylight in the morning and afternoon. This helps establish their circadian rhythm earlier.
translations.sleep_tip_0_3m_feeding: Newborns feed every 2–3 hours. Feeding is part of sleep — do not try to stretch feeds to improve sleep at this stage.
translations.sleep_tip_0_3m_expectations: Sleeping through the night is not realistic before 3 months. Focus on recognising sleep cues, not on schedules.
translations.sleep_tip_0_3m_drowsy: Practice putting your baby down drowsy but awake as often as you can. It will not work every time — keep practicing.
translations.sleep_tip_4_6m_regression: The 4-month regression is permanent — the brain changes. The most effective response is consistent sleep associations and a predictable bedtime routine.
translations.sleep_tip_4_6m_consistent_time: Aim for a consistent bedtime between 7:00–8:00 PM. Overtiredness at this age makes falling asleep harder, not easier.
translations.sleep_tip_4_6m_drowsy: Put your baby down drowsy but awake at every sleep opportunity — naps and bedtime. This builds the self-soothing skill gradually.
translations.sleep_tip_7_9m_separation_anxiety: Separation anxiety peaks around 8–10 months. This is developmentally normal. Consistent goodbye rituals and prompt, calm responses to night waking help.
translations.sleep_tip_7_9m_regression_8_10m: The 8–10 month regression coincides with major motor milestones. Expect 2–6 weeks of disrupted sleep. Maintain your routine.
translations.sleep_tip_7_9m_consistent_schedule: Keep wake time, nap times, and bedtime within a 30-minute window every day — including weekends.
translations.sleep_tip_10_12m_nap_transition_signs: Signs the 2-nap schedule is working: baby wakes happy from both naps, falls asleep within 20 minutes at bedtime, and sleeps 10–12 hours at night.
translations.sleep_tip_10_12m_separation_anxiety_peak: Separation anxiety is still high. A comfort object (lovey) approved for this age can significantly reduce night waking.
translations.sleep_tip_10_12m_no_nap_after_4pm: Avoid letting your baby nap after 4:00 PM — it pushes bedtime too late and fragments night sleep.
translations.sleep_tip_13_18m_nap_transition_2_1: Signs your toddler is ready for 1 nap: fighting the morning nap, taking over 30 minutes to fall asleep at nap time, or nighttime sleep disruption. The transition takes 4–8 weeks.
translations.sleep_tip_13_18m_overtired_signs: An overtired toddler is harder to put down. Signs: rubbing eyes, pulling ears, clinginess, reduced activity, losing interest in play.
translations.sleep_tip_13_18m_molars: First molars erupt between 13–19 months and can temporarily disrupt sleep. A cool teething ring before bed and age-appropriate pain relief (consult your doctor) may help.
translations.sleep_tip_13_18m_bedtime_7_8pm: Ideal bedtime for this age is 7:00–8:00 PM. Later bedtimes create an overtired cycle that makes waking earlier, not sleeping later.
translations.sleep_tip_19_24m_nightmares_begin: Nightmares begin appearing around 18–24 months as imagination grows. Comfort quickly, keep the interaction brief and calm, and avoid turning on bright lights.
translations.sleep_tip_19_24m_stalling_tactics: Toddlers are skilled at bedtime stalling ('one more hug', 'water', 'scared'). A defined routine with a clear endpoint reduces stalling significantly.
translations.sleep_tip_19_24m_consistent_wake_time: A consistent wake time (within 30 minutes daily) anchors all other sleep. It is more powerful than consistent bedtime alone.
translations.sleep_tip_19_24m_2yr_regression: The 2-year regression is tied to language explosion, new awareness of the world, and testing limits. It typically lasts 2–6 weeks. Stay consistent.
translations.sleep_tip_25_36m_quiet_time_no_nap: If your child drops the nap, replace it with 45–60 minutes of quiet independent play in their room. Their brain still needs the rest even if they do not sleep.
translations.sleep_tip_25_36m_nighttime_fears: Fears of the dark, monsters, or separation become common after 2.5 years. Validate the fear without reinforcing avoidance. A dim nightlight is appropriate.
translations.sleep_tip_25_36m_3yr_regression: The 3-year regression is linked to emotional and cognitive growth. Increased nightmares, bedtime resistance, and early waking are common for 2–6 weeks.
translations.sleep_tip_25_36m_potty_training_impact: Potty training can temporarily disrupt sleep — your child may wake to use the toilet or feel anxious about accidents. Overnight pull-ups are completely fine until age 4.
translations.str_desc_swaddle: Wrapping your newborn snugly in a thin blanket mimics the feeling of the womb and reduces the startle (Moro) reflex that wakes young babies. Use a breathable muslin blanket.
translations.str_step_swaddle_1: Lay the blanket in a diamond, fold the top corner down 15 cm.
translations.str_step_swaddle_2: Place baby with neck at the fold. Wrap left side across, tuck under. Wrap right side across, tuck under.
translations.str_step_swaddle_3: Ensure hips are loose — there must be room for legs to bend up and out (hip-healthy position).
translations.str_why_swaddle: The Moro reflex is fully present until 3–4 months. Swaddling suppresses it and reduces night wakings caused by involuntary arm movements. Evidence: AAP-endorsed for newborns when applied correctly.
translations.str_warn_swaddle_hips: Warning: Never swaddle legs straight and tight — this increases the risk of hip dysplasia. Hips must have room to flex.
translations.str_warn_swaddle_rolling: Stop swaddling as soon as your baby shows any signs of rolling (usually 3–4 months). A swaddled rolling baby is a suffocation risk.
translations.str_desc_white_noise: Consistent low-level background sound masks household noise and replicates the constant sound environment of the womb.
translations.str_why_white_noise: Studies show white noise can reduce infant crying by up to 80% and shorten sleep onset time. It also helps mask sudden sounds (door slam, sibling) that cause night waking.
translations.str_warn_white_noise_volume: Warning: Keep white noise below 50 dB measured at the baby's ear, and place the device at least 60 cm from the crib. Sustained loud white noise can cause hearing damage.
translations.str_desc_drowsy_awake: Instead of letting your baby fall fully asleep in your arms, put them down when their eyes are drooping but they are not fully asleep. This helps them learn to fall asleep independently.
translations.str_step_drowsy_1: Complete your sleep cue routine (feed, song, dim lights).
translations.str_step_drowsy_2: Watch for heavy eyelids and slowing movements — this is the drowsy window.
translations.str_step_drowsy_3: Place baby in the crib, put a gentle hand on their chest briefly, then step back.
translations.str_why_drowsy_awake: When a baby always falls asleep being held or fed, they expect those conditions when they surface between sleep cycles at night. Falling asleep independently means they can re-settle without waking you. This is the single most evidence-backed sleep strategy across all ages.
translations.str_desc_feed_wake_sleep: Structure each 'awake window' as: feed first, then alert play, then back to sleep. This breaks the feed-to-sleep association that causes frequent night waking.
translations.str_why_feed_wake_sleep: When feeding is the last event before sleep, the baby needs to be fed again to return to sleep each night waking. Separating feeding from sleep onset reduces this dependency.
translations.str_desc_bedtime_routine: A predictable sequence of 4–6 calming steps done in the same order every night. Aim for 20–30 minutes total.
translations.str_step_routine_bath: Warm bath (10 minutes, optional but highly effective — body temperature drop after bath triggers sleepiness).
translations.str_step_routine_feed: Quiet feed — breast or bottle. Keep lights dim.
translations.str_step_routine_song: One lullaby or gentle song in a darkened room.
translations.str_step_routine_book: One short board book read quietly (for toddlers 13 months+).
translations.str_step_routine_dark_room: Move to the sleep space. Blackout curtains closed. White noise on if used.
translations.str_step_routine_put_down: Say your consistent goodnight phrase. Place baby down drowsy but awake. Leave the room.
translations.str_why_bedtime_routine: Routines work by conditioning. After 2–3 weeks, the routine itself triggers melatonin release. Research shows babies with consistent routines fall asleep faster, wake less at night, and have longer total sleep.
translations.str_desc_sleep_training_intro: Sleep training refers to methods that help babies learn to fall asleep independently. The most evidence-supported methods are graduated extinction (Ferber) and extinction (Weissbluth). All are safe when the baby is healthy and over 4 months.
translations.str_why_sleep_training: Multiple large systematic reviews (AAP 2020, Cochrane) confirm sleep training does not harm attachment, mental health, stress hormones, or parent-child relationships. It effectively improves infant and maternal sleep.
translations.str_warn_sleep_training_consult_ped: Always discuss sleep training with your pediatrician before starting, especially if your baby has reflux, weight concerns, or other medical conditions.
translations.str_desc_consistent_bedtime: Put your baby to bed within the same 30-minute window every night, ideally between 7:00–8:00 PM.
translations.str_why_consistent_bedtime: Circadian rhythm is governed by consistent light and time cues. A consistent bedtime synchronises the body clock, shortens sleep onset, and deepens night sleep.
translations.str_desc_wake_window_4_6m: At 4–6 months, babies can comfortably stay awake for 90–120 minutes. Exceeding this leads to cortisol release, making sleep onset harder — not easier.
translations.str_why_wake_window: Overtiredness creates a cortisol surge that counteracts adenosine (sleep pressure). A well-timed nap on the wake window keeps the stress system calm and makes falling asleep easier.
translations.str_desc_object_permanence: From 7 months, babies understand that objects (and people) exist when out of sight. This cognitive leap causes separation anxiety but also means comfort strategies now work better.
translations.str_step_op_peek_boo: Play peekaboo games during awake time — this directly teaches 'gone does not mean forever.'
translations.str_step_op_consistent_farewell: Use the same short goodbye phrase every time you leave the room at bedtime. Consistency reduces anxiety.
translations.str_step_op_comfort_object: Introduce a small, breathable comfort object (lovey) — a soft cloth or small stuffed animal. Begin by sleeping with it yourself so it carries your scent.
translations.str_why_object_permanence: Understanding object permanence is what makes sleep training and independent sleep possible at this age. It also means separation anxiety needs to be acknowledged, not ignored.
translations.str_desc_self_soothing: Self-soothing is the ability to transition between sleep cycles without parental intervention. It is a skill, not an instinct — it must be learned through opportunity.
translations.str_why_self_soothing: Babies naturally wake 3–6 times per night between sleep cycles. A baby who can self-soothe returns to sleep independently. One who cannot signals for a parent each time. Most sleep problems in the first 2 years are self-soothing issues, not medical ones.
translations.str_warn_self_soothing_readiness: Not all babies are ready at the same age. Signs of readiness: no medical conditions, weight appropriate for age, over 4 months corrected age. Always discuss with your pediatrician.
translations.str_desc_comfort_object: A small, breathable cloth or stuffed animal that becomes associated with sleep and security. Safe for use from 7 months under supervision, from 12 months independently in the crib.
translations.str_why_comfort_object: A comfort object acts as a transitional object — it provides security in the absence of a caregiver. Research shows it reduces separation anxiety and night waking without creating dependency.
translations.str_warn_comfort_object_aap: Note: AAP safe sleep guidelines recommend keeping soft objects out of the crib for infants under 12 months. Introduce the lovey at 7+ months under supervision only. After 12 months it is safe to leave in the crib.
translations.str_desc_separation_anxiety: Separation anxiety peaks at 10–18 months and again around 2 years. It is a sign of healthy attachment. The goal is to comfort and reassure while still supporting independent sleep.
translations.str_step_sep_consistent_goodbye: Keep the goodbye brief and consistent — the same 5–10 second phrase and action every night. Long, drawn-out goodbyes increase anxiety.
translations.str_step_sep_comfort_object: Place the comfort object in the crib before putting your child down.
translations.str_step_sep_short_checks: If using a check-in method, keep checks short (under 60 seconds), calm, and with the same phrase. Avoid picking up unless genuinely distressed.
translations.str_why_separation_anxiety: Inconsistent responses (sometimes picking up, sometimes not) prolong sleep difficulties. Predictability is comforting — your child learns 'when I put them down, they come back.' Consistency is compassion here.
translations.str_desc_graduated_ext: Put your baby to bed drowsy but awake. If they cry, wait a set interval before checking. Gradually increase the wait time over nights. Check-ins are brief and calm — no picking up.
translations.str_why_graduated_ext: Graduated extinction is the most studied sleep training method. Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses show it is safe, effective, and does not cause long-term emotional harm. Typical results: sleep improvement within 3–7 nights.
translations.str_warn_sleep_training_consult_ped: Always discuss sleep training with your pediatrician before starting.
translations.str_desc_nap_transition_2_1: Most babies are ready between 14–18 months, though readiness varies. The transition takes 4–8 weeks. Do not rush — an early transition leads to chronic overtiredness.
translations.str_step_nap21_push_morning: Begin by pushing the morning nap 15 minutes later every 3 days until it reaches noon.
translations.str_step_nap21_early_bedtime: On days when only one nap happens, move bedtime 30–45 minutes earlier to prevent overtiredness.
translations.str_step_nap21_watch_signs: Watch for: nap refusal 3+ days a week, taking over 30 minutes to fall asleep at nap time, sleeping at bedtime in under 5 minutes (too tired).
translations.str_why_nap_transition: Dropping a nap before the child is biologically ready creates cumulative sleep debt that shows as behaviour problems, night waking, and early rising.
translations.str_warn_nap_transition_overtired: Warning: On days when the transition nap is skipped, move bedtime earlier. An overtired toddler will not sleep better — they will sleep worse.
translations.str_desc_limit_setting: From 13 months, toddlers begin testing boundaries. Clear, kind, and consistent responses to bedtime boundary-testing (calling out, getting up) are more effective than permissive responses.
translations.str_why_limit_setting: Toddlers feel safer with predictable, consistent limits. The 'chair method' and 'camping out' techniques are gentle versions that work well at this age.
translations.str_desc_visual_schedule: A sequence of 4–6 picture cards showing the bedtime routine in order. Posted on the wall where your toddler can see them.
translations.str_step_vs_create_cards: Create or print simple picture cards: bath, pyjamas, teeth, book, song, bed.
translations.str_step_vs_involve_child: Let your toddler flip or tick off each card as it is completed. Giving them agency reduces resistance.
translations.str_step_vs_follow_sequence: Always follow the same sequence. Predictability is the point.
translations.str_why_visual_schedule: Toddlers between 18–30 months respond strongly to visual routines because their verbal comprehension exceeds expression. Seeing the routine reduces negotiation and power struggles.
translations.str_desc_bedtime_stalling: Bedtime stalling ('one more hug', 'I'm thirsty', 'I'm scared') peaks between 2–4 years. It is developmentally normal and manageable.
translations.str_why_bedtime_stalling: Pre-empt most requests: offer water before bed, do a final hug during the routine, check for monsters together. Then state clearly: 'Everything is done. It is time to sleep.' Consistent follow-through within 1–2 weeks reduces stalling significantly.
translations.str_desc_nightmares: Nightmares become common from 18 months as imagination develops. Respond calmly, briefly, and avoid over-stimulating the child.
translations.str_why_nightmares: Go to the child, offer brief calm comfort, use a reassuring phrase, leave the room. Avoid long stays, co-sleeping unless culturally intended, or over-dramatising the nightmare. Brief, consistent comfort is most effective.
translations.str_desc_quiet_time: When the afternoon nap is dropped, replace it with 45–60 minutes of quiet, independent activity in a calm space.
translations.str_step_qt_define_space: Designate a quiet, safe space — ideally the child's room. Place a few calm toys or books within reach.
translations.str_step_qt_set_timer: Use a visual timer (sand timer or ok-to-wake clock). Start with 20 minutes and build gradually to 45–60.
translations.str_step_qt_calm_activities: Books, puzzles, colouring. No screens. No loud or active play.
translations.str_why_quiet_time: Even without sleep, rest reduces cortisol, prevents overtiredness, and supports afternoon behaviour. Children who have quiet time show better mood, attention, and sleep quality at night.
translations.str_desc_bedtime_fears: Fear of the dark, monsters, and being alone becomes common from 2.5–3 years. Fears are real to the child — dismissing them increases anxiety.
translations.str_step_fears_validate: Acknowledge the fear: 'I know the dark can feel scary. You are safe.'
translations.str_step_fears_monster_spray: 'Monster spray' (water in a small spray bottle) can be playfully effective — let your child spray the room.
translations.str_step_fears_nightlight: A dim, warm-toned nightlight is appropriate and does not disrupt melatonin significantly.
translations.str_step_fears_comfort_object: Ensure the comfort object is in the bed. 'Teddy will stay with you all night.'
translations.str_why_bedtime_fears: Validating without reinforcing avoidance is key. Brief, consistent check-ins ('I will check on you in 5 minutes') build confidence without rewarding fear-driven waking.
translations.str_desc_sleep_clock: A colour-changing clock or timer that shows the child visually when it is okay to get out of bed (usually turns green).
translations.str_why_sleep_clock: Toddlers over 2 years lack the ability to read time but respond very well to visual signals. An OK-to-wake clock can reduce early waking and pre-dawn room visits significantly within 1–2 weeks of consistent use.
translations.env_temp_why: Temperature is one of the strongest environmental signals for sleep. Babies sleep best in a cool room. Overheating is a known risk factor for SIDS (Sudden Infant Death Syndrome).
translations.env_temp_tip_neck_check: Check the back of the baby's neck — it should feel warm but not sweaty. Sweating = too hot.
translations.env_temp_tip_no_fan_direct: Do not point a fan directly at the baby. Use it to circulate room air instead.
translations.env_temp_tip_one_layer: A general rule: dress your baby in one more layer than you are comfortable in.
translations.env_temp_tip_light_blanket_ok: A light blanket is safe from approximately 12 months, once the child can move it themselves.
translations.env_temp_warn_overheating_sids: Medical warning: Overheating is a significant independent risk factor for SIDS. Keep room temperature between 18–21°C (64–70°F) at all times during sleep.
translations.env_light_why_melatonin: Melatonin (the sleep hormone) is suppressed by light, especially blue-spectrum light. Darkness at bedtime triggers melatonin onset; morning light stops it. This is the primary mechanism governing the circadian rhythm.
translations.env_light_day_bright: Bright natural light during the day
translations.env_light_night_soft_optional: Complete darkness preferred; soft dim nightlight acceptable if child is fearful
translations.env_light_tip_blackout: Use blackout curtains or blinds. Light leaking around curtain edges is enough to delay melatonin onset, especially in summer.
translations.env_light_tip_no_led: Cover LED indicator lights on baby monitors, humidifiers, and other devices.
translations.env_light_tip_dim_red_diaper: For night nappy changes, use a dim red-tinted light. Red light does not suppress melatonin as strongly as white or blue light.
translations.env_light_tip_nightlight_ok_13m: A very dim nightlight is acceptable from 13 months if the child shows signs of fear of the dark. Use amber or red tones, not white or blue.
translations.env_light_tip_nightlight_fears: A dim warm-toned nightlight is appropriate for toddlers who are fearful of the dark. Keep it below the level of direct eye contact.
translations.env_light_warn_blue_light: Warning: Blue-spectrum light (phones, tablets, TVs) strongly suppresses melatonin and delays sleep onset by 30–90 minutes. No screens within 60 minutes of bedtime.
translations.env_light_warn_blue_light_screens: Warning: Screen exposure within 60 minutes of bedtime delays sleep onset significantly in toddlers. Establish a clear 'no screens after X' rule and keep it consistent.
translations.env_noise_why: Sudden sounds during light sleep cycles cause partial or full waking. Continuous background sound (white noise) masks these spikes and maintains a consistent sound environment.
translations.env_noise_white_noise_ok: White noise recommended — gentle, consistent level
translations.env_noise_white_noise_optional: White noise optional — use if helpful for your child
translations.env_noise_quiet_preferred: Quiet room preferred; low white noise acceptable if established
translations.env_noise_tip_consistent: Once white noise is established as a sleep cue, use it consistently at every sleep. Removing it is a change the baby must adjust to.
translations.env_noise_tip_distance_60cm: Place the white noise device at least 60 cm (2 feet) from the crib. Measure volume at the crib — not at the device.
translations.env_noise_tip_white_noise_optional: White noise is habit-forming. If you choose to use it, plan to phase it out gradually between 2–3 years.
translations.env_noise_warn_volume_hearing: Warning: White noise above 50 dB at the baby's ear level can cause hearing damage with prolonged exposure. The AAP recommends a maximum of 50 dB. Most sleep machines at 2 metres are well below this — check the label.
translations.env_surface_why_sids: Soft surfaces (mattresses, sofas, adult beds) allow a baby's face to sink in, restricting the airway. Firm, flat surfaces keep the airway clear. This is the most critical safe sleep factor.
translations.env_surface_why_toddler: Once a child can move independently, surface rigidity is less critical. The priority shifts to fall safety and appropriate bed transitions.
translations.env_surface_firm_flat: Firm, flat, approved sleep surface (crib, bassinet, or pack-n-play)
translations.env_surface_crib_toddler_bed: Crib (preferred) or toddler bed when ready
translations.env_surface_toddler_or_twin_bed: Toddler bed or small twin bed with bed rail
translations.env_surface_tip_aap_approved: Use only a crib, bassinet, or play yard that meets current safety standards (no drop-side cribs, no inclined sleepers, no sleep pods).
translations.env_surface_tip_no_soft_bedding: Remove all soft bedding, bumpers, pillows, positioners, and toys from the sleep space for all babies under 12 months.
translations.env_surface_tip_no_bumpers: Crib bumpers — including 'breathable' mesh bumpers — are not recommended. They pose entrapment and suffocation risks without reducing injury.
translations.env_surface_tip_no_incline: Inclined sleepers (more than 10 degrees) are not safe. The AAP and CPSC issued firm warnings following infant deaths in inclined products.
translations.env_surface_tip_crib_still_safe: A crib with a firm mattress remains the safest sleep surface even through the toddler years. Transition to a toddler bed only when the child can climb out of the crib.
translations.env_surface_tip_toddler_bed_transition_s: Signs it is time for a toddler bed: the child climbs out repeatedly (fall risk), or has reached the crib's height limit.
translations.env_surface_tip_toddler_bed_when_climbin: Transition to a toddler bed when the child begins climbing out of the crib — falls are the primary injury risk at this stage.
translations.env_surface_tip_pillow_ok_after_2yr: A small, flat pillow is safe from approximately 2 years. Use a toddler-sized pillow, not an adult pillow.
translations.env_surface_tip_light_blanket_ok: A light blanket is safe from 12 months. Ensure the child can move it off their face independently.
translations.env_surface_tip_bed_rail_safety: If using a toddler bed or converted crib without full rails, add a bed rail or place the mattress on the floor temporarily.
translations.env_surface_warn_no_sofa: Warning: Never let a baby sleep on a sofa, armchair, or adult bed unsupervised. These are the locations of the majority of sleep-related infant deaths.
translations.env_surface_warn_no_inclined_sleeper: Warning: Inclined sleepers, bouncers, and car seat use for sleep outside the car are associated with infant deaths. The AAP advises against their use for sleep.
translations.env_surface_warn_no_pillows: Warning: No pillows, rolled blankets, or positioning wedges in the crib for babies under 12 months.
translations.env_surface_warn_no_pillow_until_2yr: Note: Pillows are not recommended until at least 2 years of age.
translations.env_position_back_only: Always on their back — every sleep, every time
translations.env_position_back_or_self_7m: Start on their back. If baby rolls independently to their front, it is safe to leave them.
translations.env_position_self_rolling_ok: Baby may sleep in any position they roll to independently
translations.env_position_why_sids: Back sleeping reduces SIDS risk by 50%. This recommendation has been in place since 1994. The rate of SIDS deaths dropped by over 50% following the 'Back to Sleep' campaign.
translations.env_position_why_7m_rolling: Once a baby can roll both ways (back to front and front to back), it is safe to leave them in whatever position they roll to during sleep. Repositioning a rolling baby repeatedly is not necessary and disrupts sleep.
translations.env_position_why_self_rolling: Once rolling both directions is established, independent repositioning is safe and normal.
translations.env_position_why_toddler: Toddlers naturally choose their sleep position. Position is not a safety concern after 12 months.
translations.env_position_tip_tummy_time_awake: Tummy time is essential for motor development — do it when the baby is awake and supervised, not during sleep.
translations.env_position_tip_supervised_only: Tummy time on the chest of a fully awake adult is fine. Never allow a baby to sleep on an adult's chest while the adult is sleeping or drowsy.
translations.env_position_tip_rolling_note_4_6m: As your baby approaches rolling (4–6 months), they may roll to their side during sleep. Gently reposition to back. Once they roll both ways fully, repositioning is not needed.
translations.env_position_warn_back_until_1yr: Medical note: The back-sleeping recommendation applies for all sleep until 12 months, even once babies can roll independently in the early months.
translations.env_room_share_optional_after_6m: Optional: continue room sharing or transition to own room
translations.env_room_share_optional: Parent choice: room sharing or own room both appropriate
translations.env_room_share_why: The AAP recommends room-sharing (baby in your room, in their own safe sleep space) for at least the first 6 months, ideally 1 year. Room sharing reduces SIDS risk by up to 50% compared to sleeping in a separate room.
translations.env_room_share_toddler_why: After 12 months, room sharing or separate room are both appropriate. The priority shifts to ensuring the child's sleep space is safe and that parental presence is consistent and predictable.
translations.env_room_share_tip_same_room_diff_surfac: Room sharing means your baby sleeps in your room — in their own crib or bassinet. It does not mean sharing a bed, sofa, or chair.
translations.env_room_share_tip_own_room_ok_after_6m: Transitioning to their own room after 6 months is safe and appropriate. Some families find both parent and baby sleep better with a room transition.
translations.env_room_share_tip_own_room_ok: Their own room is safe and often improves sleep for both baby and parents.
translations.env_room_share_warn_no_bed_sharing: Warning: Bed sharing (co-sleeping) on an adult mattress with pillows and blankets is not recommended by the AAP. If you do choose to bed share, remove all soft bedding and pillows, never do so after alcohol or sedative medication, and never on a sofa or armchair.
translations.evidence_strong_desc: Supported by multiple randomised controlled trials, systematic reviews, and endorsed by major medical bodies (AAP, WHO).
translations.evidence_moderate_desc: Supported by observational studies, expert consensus, or clinical guidelines. Effective in practice with limited RCT data.
translations.evidence_emerging_desc: Promising but limited research. Based primarily on clinical experience and expert opinion.
